title: The College-Educated Working Class
content:
This is an age of mutinies, and for more than a decade in America, they have come thick and fast. Movements ranging from the Tea Party and Occupy to Black Lives Matter and the Resistance have continually tripped over one another. The ultimate mutiny encompassing some of these forces is MAGA, which acts like a rioter laying dynamite at the foundation of a decayed establishment. We often understand these revolts in terms of the dominant political fact of our time: the forever war between red and blue. These mutinies divide Americans instantly and predictably into two opposing camps.

These camps hold irreconcilable visions of what the country is and should be: multicultural America versus heritage America. The former is inclusive, outward- and forward-looking, while the latter is exclusive, inward-looking, and nostalgic for a past it tries to recapture. The obvious precedent for this era is the decade before the Civil War, when pressure built until it exploded in an irrepressible conflict. Now that masked militias are battling residents in the streets, our own conflict seems to be coming to a head. However, if we unfasten our gaze from the prospect of another civil war, a different historical period comes to mind.

The fundamental sources of our troubles, going back half a century, are economic inequality, political paralysis, mass immigration, and cultural upheavals. These were exactly the country’s great problems at the start of the previous century. In 1914, Walter Lippmann wrote that we are all immigrants in the industrial world, with no authority to lean upon. Today, the education divide is the most salient characteristic of our new era. We have abandoned physical reality for mostly online battles, struggling to navigate an increasingly fractured society.
